edge: Activation functions and their motivation are key considerations within the neural network community.

Sigmoidal activation functions are no longer commonly used within machine learning between layers of representations.
Sigmoid is still widely used for gating operations which scale the magnitude of other features in an attention-like manner.
The primary disadvantage of the sigmoid activation function is its vanishing gradient.
As the potentiation rises, activity converges to a plateau.
Activity convergence to a plateau causes the gradient to go to zero.
This prevents feedback information propagating back through the network from the loss function to the early layers of the network.

ReLU(x) = max(0, x), does not have this problem, since in its non-zero regime it has a gradient of 1. Another advantage of ReLU is it has very low computational demands. Since it is both effective and efficient, it has proven to be a highly popular choice of activation function.
Variants of ReLU have emerged, aiming to smooth out its transition between domains and provide a gradient in its inactive regime. These include ELU (Clevert et al., 2016).
Variants of ReLU have emerged, aiming to smooth out its transition between domains and provide a gradient in its inactive regime. These include CELU (Barron, 2017).
Variants of ReLU have emerged, aiming to smooth out its transition between domains and provide a gradient in its inactive regime. These include SELU (Klambauer et al., 2017).
Variants of ReLU have emerged, aiming to smooth out its transition between domains and provide a gradient in its inactive regime. These include GELU (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2020).
Variants of ReLU have emerged, aiming to smooth out its transition between domains and provide a gradient in its inactive regime. These include SiLU (Elfwing et al., 2018; Ramachandran et al., 2017).
Variants of ReLU have emerged, aiming to smooth out its transition between domains and provide a gradient in its inactive regime. These include Mish (Misra, 2019).

Logical reasoning may benefit from activation functions applying logical operations to logits.
Manipulation of probabilities in logit-space is known to be more efficient for many calculations.
The log-odds form of Bayes’ Rule states that the posterior logit equals the prior logit plus the log of the likelihood ratio for the new evidence.
Working in logit-space allows for performing Bayesian updates on many sources of evidence simultaneously.
Bayesian updates can be performed on multiple sources of evidence by summing together the log-likelihood ratios.
A weighted sum may be used if the amount of credence given to the sources differs.
a linear layer in a neural network performs a weighted sum operation based on different sources.

ANDAIL is a linear-approximate AND function for independent logits, while ANDIL is an approximate IL function. ANDAIL(x, y) := x + y, min(x, y), otherwise x < 0, y < 0.
ANDAIL is a constructed approximation of the logit-space probabilistic-and function for independent logits. It can be compared to the max function.
ORIL is the logit-space OR function for independent logits, which can be roughly approximated by the max function.
ORIL is the exact logit-space probabilistic-or function for independent logits, while ORAIL is an approximation for the upper-right quadrant. ORAIL(x, y) := x + y, max(x, y), otherwise x > 0, y > 0.

The majority of activation functions are one-dimensional, while the proposed activation functions are all two-dimensional.
The proposed activation functions are all two-dimensional, applied to pairs of features from the embedding space, and will reduce the dimensionality of the input space by a factor of 2.
MaxOut networks refer to networks using max as their activation function, similar to the proposed activation functions.
MaxOut can be considered as a model for two-dimensional activation functions, as both use max as their activation function.
The proposed activation functions could be generalized to higher dimensional inputs by considering the behavior of logit-space AND, OR, XNOR operations.
By using multiple logit-Boolean activation functions, the network can have multiple options on how to relate features together.
When combining the activation functions, two strategies were considered: one of them is the partition strategy.
Partitions are used to apply different activation functions, resulting in concatenated results.
Under this strategy, the output dimension is always half that of the input for each AIL activation function.
M different activation functions are applied in parallel to the same elements, resulting in larger output with dimension m nc.
Using two AIL activation functions together can counteract the 2 → 1 reduction in output.
A negative weight in the network is equivalent to the logit-NOT operator.
A multi-layer network using only the ORIL activation function should be able to replicate any probabilistic logic circuit.
Utilizing these activation functions simultaneously allows networks to access logit-space equivalents of 12 of the 16 Boolean logical operations.
Including bias term and skip connections gives the network access to logit-space equivalents of all 16 Boolean logical operations.
Training the network with all three activation functions in an ensemble could yield better results by avoiding the need for additional layers.
The network would not need to expend layers having to combine ORIL operations together to yield other Boolean operations.
